questions.scenario: The team has a normal Node.js checkout API. It receives HTTPS requests all day, needs app settings, reads secrets, and should give the team logs and health checks without managing a full server. Which first runtime shape fits best?

options.text: Azure Functions, because every backend can be split into event handlers later.
Azure Virtual Machines, because a server gives the most control by default.
Azure App Service or Azure Container Apps, because the team is bringing application code or a container and wants Azure to own much of the runtime work.
Azure Storage, because the service mostly stores order data after each request.

questions.explanation: The article separates bringing code or a container from bringing a machine. A steady API usually wants a managed application runtime first. Functions and VMs can be right for specific cases, but they change what the team owns.

questions.scenario: The same product also has a nightly export job, a receipt sender triggered by queue messages, and one legacy worker that requires a custom Linux package. Which pairings are reasonable starting points?

options.text: Use Azure Functions for the queue-triggered receipt sender.
Use a VM for the legacy worker if the custom package and OS control are real requirements.
Use one large VM for everything because shared hosting reduces the number of Azure services.
Use Container Apps for a packaged API if the team already ships a container image.

questions.explanation: Different jobs can use different compute choices. The question is what each workload needs at runtime, not how to force every job into one hosting model.

questions.scenario: A deployment succeeds, but the new API copy never becomes ready. The build artifact is the expected version. What evidence should the team collect before changing the runtime service?

options.text: Resource names only, because names prove the deployment reached Azure.
The runtime image or code version, app settings, identity, health check path, recent logs, and scale or revision state.
The developer machine output, because the same code passed locally.
A list of all Azure services in the subscription, because the failure might be anywhere.

questions.explanation: Compute failures usually sit at the boundary between artifact, configuration, identity, health, logs, and scaling. A broad inventory or local result is less useful than direct runtime evidence.
